suggestions.originalText: Frazee (Midwest finesse): Rig the 3-inch Ika on a 1/32-oz mushroom head jig three ways — as a solid-body tube (tentacles as tail), reversed (tentacles at collar), or wacky style
suggestions.reasoning: Pure rigging technique — three ways to rig Ika on mushroom head. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Frazee (Midwest finesse): Retrieve the 3-inch Ika with all six standard Midwest finesse retrieves or a jig-and-bobber presentation
suggestions.reasoning: Pure retrieve technique — six standard Midwest finesse retrieves. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: DeFronzo: Rig the 3-inch Ika on a 1/16 or 1/8-oz Florida rig screw-in weight with a 2/0 hook — creates a downsized Mega Tube look; skip through brush, trees, and docks; let fall on controlled slack line with a hop or two before retrieving
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique for Florida rig. No conditions mentioned.

suggestions.originalText: DeFronzo: For extremely shallow water or over bottom-growing vegetation, omit weight entirely and rig the Ika on a 2/0 or 3/0 hook — the slow descent lets every bass in the area scope it out
suggestions.reasoning: 'Extremely shallow water' and 'over bottom-growing vegetation' implies very shallow depths.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Fukae: Rig the 3-inch Fat Senko on a drop-shot with a very short 3-inch leader to replicate a Ned Rig — 'This is just like a Ned Rig but is better around cover' since standard Ned Rigs hang up on the bottom
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — drop-shot with short leader to replicate Ned Rig around cover. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: A 3-inch Senko on Ned head is ideal where small bluegill abound — can also trim a 5-6 inch Senko to fit
suggestions.reasoning: General bank fishing tip about bait selection. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'I like the 3-inch Fat Senko for my Ned Rigs'
suggestions.reasoning: Pure bait preference — no conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto Yamatanuki is a heavy plastic (5/8oz without weight) that is a cross between weightless Senko and Ned rig. Fish on 5/0 BKK Nemesis EWG hook or BKK Silent Chaser 4/0 standup head as a power Ned
suggestions.reasoning: Product description of Yamatanuki. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Rig Yamatanuki with hook point coming out of big end and small end acting as tail for more action and better hookups
suggestions.reasoning: Pure rigging technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: color "Smoke/Black Blue Gold Flake (214)" (hex: #696969)
suggestions.reasoning: No context for when to use this color.

suggestions.originalText: color "Green Pumpkin (297)" (hex: #8B7D3C)
suggestions.reasoning: Green pumpkin is a versatile all-around color. No specific conditions.

suggestions.originalText: color "Smoke with Red Flake (334)" (hex: #808080)
suggestions.reasoning: No context for when to use this color.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: The Zako was designed as a chatter-style bait trailer; its restrained action won't overpower the vibrating jig — most other soft plastic trailers exhibit too much action or aren't durable enough
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer selection advice — Zako designed for ChatterBait. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Using a stick bait as vibrating jig trailer shows finicky fish a more streamlined profile — something different
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer profile tip for finicky fish. No specific conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: The Yamamoto Zako is the perfect ChatterBait trailer — bigger profile, good but subtle action, and an 'accordion-style' tail that collapses when fish bite, increasing hook-up ratio
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer description — Zako features. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'I like the regular Zako on my Jack Hammer ChatterBaits — the ChatterBait has so much vibration and gives the Zako a nice natural action'
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer preference. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto Zako is the go-to ChatterBait trailer
suggestions.reasoning: Simple trailer recommendation. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Fish an unweighted Senko on a wide-gap hook across the surface — hopping and skipping like a fleeing minnow or bream; works best on heavily pressured fish that have seen every other lure
suggestions.reasoning: 'Across the surface' implies topwater/extremely shallow. 'Heavily pressured fish' is situational, not a condition field.

suggestions.originalText: Use 30-lb braid for heavy weeds with a 7:1 baitcaster on a 7'3" MH rod; switch to 15-lb fluoro for open water
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup tip — braid for weeds, fluoro for open water. No weather/seasonal conditions.

suggestions.originalText: In open water, use a fast spinning reel with 10-lb braid on a 7' medium-action rod for the surface Senko technique
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup for surface Senko technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: If a bass chases the surface Senko, just stop it and let it sit — fish will grab it on the fall even though it doesn't look great sinking
suggestions.reasoning: Retrieve technique — what to do when bass chases. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Yelas: Use at least a 1/4-oz weight with the Cowboy on a Texas rig because the bait is so bulky — 3/8-oz or heavier is ideal to get it down
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging weight advice for Cowboy bait. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: Rig a Senko on a weighted screw-lock hook to cover water much quicker than wacky rigging or weightless — maintains excellent skipping attributes while adding speed
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — weighted screw-lock for speed. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: On Lake Champlain, Gary Yamamoto dragged a weighted Texas-rigged Senko across the bottom instead of the standard lift-reel technique — the dragging action was something smallmouth couldn't resist, possibly imitating a crawfish
suggestions.reasoning: Specific technique on Lake Champlain — dragging on bottom. Location-specific but no standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Schultz: Texas-rigged Senkos are excellent flipping baits — use a 5/16-oz bullet weight with a 3/0 or 4/0 VMC wide gap offset worm hook on a 4- or 5-inch Senko
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging/flipping technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: DeFronzo: Fish the Senko weightless Texas-rigged on a 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG hook with 6-8lb line; skip into bushes or undercut banks and let it sit — 'You can't fish it too slow'; the bait falls away from the angler like a Flying Lure Tube
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — weightless Texas rig. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: DeFronzo: Drop the weightless Senko into pockets of weeds or pads and let it flutter to the bottom; occasionally give a twitch or two but don't overdo it — bass pick it up and swim off
suggestions.reasoning: Technique for pockets of weeds/pads. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Sealock: The Senko's tail wiggles back and forth on slack line without any angler effort; its slow fall from high salt content makes it irresistible; casts like a rocket even weightless and skips easily under cover
suggestions.reasoning: Product description of Senko properties. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Sealock: Fish the Senko on a weightless Texas rig around vegetation or target-casting to stumps/laydowns — totally weedless and the tail kicks like crazy
suggestions.reasoning: General technique — weightless TX rig around vegetation. No specific conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Sealock: Use a 1/4-oz weighted Texas rig Senko for heavy cover; lighter weight is always optimal — heavier weights cause it to sink too fast, taking away from its trademark horizontal fall; let it fall on slack line as most bites come on the fall
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging weight advice for heavy cover. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Use a 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG hook — bigger hooks lose tail action on the fall and tear the bait off more often'
suggestions.reasoning: Hook selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Use smaller light-wire hooks to increase tail action and make the Senko last longer since bait slides up the hook onto the line while fighting fish'
suggestions.reasoning: Hook selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Tie with a Snell knot — when you tug on it, it pulls the hook barb straight toward the line for better hooksets'
suggestions.reasoning: Knot tying technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Use a simple lift-and-drop retrieve, like working a plastic worm but slower because it takes longer to fall'
suggestions.reasoning: Retrieve technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Use 7-foot light-action spinning rod with 2500 series reel, 10-15lb braid mainline with fluorocarbon leader'
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'The bait must hang as straight as possible on the hook, otherwise the signature fluttering action will be lost'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — bait must hang straight. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Cast to shallow cover and simply let the worm fall. The fluttering action draws the strikes. The slower you fish the Senko, the better'
suggestions.reasoning: 'Cast to shallow cover' explicitly mentions shallow. No other conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'After the bait hits bottom, let it sit for several seconds, then raise the rod tip 6 inches or a foot, then let the bait flutter down again'
suggestions.reasoning: Retrieve technique — lift and drop. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'A jarring hook set is not needed. Since the worm is skin-hooked, just reel until the line is tight and allow the fish to set the hook as it swims away'
suggestions.reasoning: Hookset technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Use 3/0 or 4/0 EWG hook, 8-12lb fluorocarbon, medium or medium-heavy rod. Fluorocarbon sinks with the bait and does not interfere with fluttering action'
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'The Yamatanuki is 3.5 inches at 5/8 oz — heavy enough to fish weightless on casting gear. It's a cross between a Senko and a jig with the profile of a small bluegill'
suggestions.reasoning: Product description of Yamatanuki. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'The Yamatanuki excels at skipping to suspended fish under docks and marinas — it's heavy enough to reach fish suspended deep in gnarly marina slips'
suggestions.reasoning: Technique for skipping under docks/marinas. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'Use a 7-foot 3-inch MH casting rod, 19lb fluorocarbon leader tied to 40lb braid, EWG hook. Emphasize hookset more than with a jig'
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: Insert a nail weight in the Senko's tail to make it enter the water like a dart and shoot under pad/mat cover — great during spring/summer rains when big fish move shallow
suggestions.reasoning: 'Spring/summer rains when big fish move shallow' — maps to spawn/post-spawn/summer seasons and shallow depth.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: For blind-casting to active baitfish activity, let Senko sink to mid-depth then use moderate retrieve speed with short rapid rod tip twitches for lots of motion
suggestions.reasoning: Retrieve technique for active baitfish. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: Use a 4/0 straight shank worm hook for most applications — slips in and out of cover well, slides across matted grass. Go 5/0 for 6-inch, 3/0 for 3-inch
suggestions.reasoning: Hook selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Elite pro Scroggins: Keep unweighted 5-inch Senko handy when topwater fishing around shallow vegetation — if a fish misses your topwater, throw the Senko at point of contact for quick followup bite
suggestions.reasoning: 'Topwater fishing around shallow vegetation' implies shallow depth.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'Rig the Fat Ika backward with an EWG hook weightless — it backslides away from you under docks, getting to places most baits can't. Also works around grass — pump your rod and it slides back away from you'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — backward Fat Ika for backsliding. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'In Florida or any big bass lake, I like the 6-inch Senko — casts a long way and you can feel it in the grass much better'
suggestions.reasoning: Bait size preference for big bass lakes. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Fish a weightless soft stickbait on braid over thick matted weeds — almost like fishing a frog, but you can let it do its wiggle in weed openings and still yard fish out of cover
suggestions.reasoning: Technique for thick matted weeds. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Peg a sinker to a stickbait for a great big-bass bait when flipping grass and penetrating into pockets where big fish roam
suggestions.reasoning: Flipping technique in grass. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Prefers weightless Texas rig with 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG hook. Some people use too big of a hook - they lose the action on the fall and the bait tears off'
suggestions.reasoning: Hook selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Smaller light-wire hooks increase tail action and make Senko last longer since bait slides easier up hook onto line while fighting fish'
suggestions.reasoning: Hook selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto: 'Recommends rigging Senko on a Yamamoto Split Shot Hook (circle hook) with a snell knot - when you tug it pulls the hook barb straight toward the line'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — hook and knot advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Howell (pro): 'Texas rig Senko on 4/0 Daiichi wide gap hook when bass are more active, work it faster'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging/retrieve for active bass. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Texas rig Senko with 3/0 or 4/0 hook and 1/8 or 3/16oz screw-on weight for flipping and pitching to bushes, brush piles, and matted vegetation
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging for flipping and pitching. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Becker (MLF pro): 'Wacky rig 5-inch Senko around docks with 7ft 2in medium-heavy spinning rod, 20lb braid mainline to 12-15lb fluorocarbon leader. Heavier leader facilitates faster sink and better durability around dock posts'
suggestions.reasoning: Gear setup for wacky rig around docks. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Becker (MLF pro): 'Position upwind of target for better casting accuracy. Target shaded regions beneath docks. Watch line movement for bites on the fall - use bright-colored braid for detection'
suggestions.reasoning: Casting/positioning technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Texas rig Senko with 3/0 Gamakatsu EWG Hook. Cast out, let sink to bottom, pick up, repeat. Action comes from the fall
suggestions.reasoning: Basic rigging and retrieve. No conditions.

suggestions.reasoning: Shad colors generally work in clearer water where bass feed on shad, but no explicit condition in text. Low confidence.

suggestions.reasoning: Green pumpkin — universal color. No conditions.

suggestions.reasoning: Green pumpkin/watermelon laminate — versatile natural color. No conditions.

suggestions.reasoning: Salt and pepper — no conditions mentioned.

suggestions.originalText: color "green pumpkin" (hex: #6B8E23)
suggestions.reasoning: Green pumpkin — universal color. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Yelas: Use the Cowboy trailer when you want a bulkier profile with more action than the Psycho Dad or Flappin' Hog, which have limited action
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer selection advice. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Shryock: The PsychoDad is his go-to around lighter cover when the bite is tough and pressured bass need an ultra-soft bait for good hookups
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer selection for tough/pressured bite. Situational but not a standard condition.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'I tear off all the tentacles on the side of the Flappin' Hog when punching mats — the larger 4.5-inch size slides through the mat better going in and coming out, making you much more efficient'
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer modification for mat punching. Technique tip, no conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto Cowboy works as flipping bait and jig trailer. Legs are sturdy but pliable enough to move water. Small appendages catch water and move with slightest shake
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer description — Cowboy features. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yama Craw with Mega Floater Formula makes buoyant jig trailer - claws float up in defensive posture mimicking real crawfish sensing danger. Great for flipping jig and football jig
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer buoyancy technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: color "black and blue" (hex: #00001A)
suggestions.reasoning: Black and blue is traditionally a stained/muddy water color, widely accepted convention.

suggestions.originalText: color "watermelon red" (hex: #4B6F44)
suggestions.reasoning: Watermelon red is typically a clearer water color but no explicit condition in text.

suggestions.originalText: color "green pumpkin" (hex: #6B8E23)
suggestions.reasoning: Green pumpkin — versatile all-around. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Yelas: 'Add a Cowboy trailer to a 3/8 or 1/2-oz swim jig to get vibration — the only real attraction of a swim jig is the skirt, the Cowboy adds kick to attract fish'
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer advice — Cowboy for vibration. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Using a stick bait as swim jig trailer shows finicky fish a more streamlined profile
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer profile tip. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'I use the Kickin' Zako almost exclusively on swim jigs — the traditional swimbait tail gets a good kicking action and helps get bigger bites. The segmented bait folds up like an accordion for better hookup'
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer preference — Kickin' Zako. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yama Craw buoyancy on swim jig allows fishing same jig slower or increasing jig weight for casting range while keeping bait at same level in water column
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer buoyancy technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Sealock: Carolina-rigging Senkos with a monofilament leader causes the Senko to float and slowly wave back and forth throughout the retrieve — great for rocky points, shell beds, river bars, and bluff walls
suggestions.reasoning: Technique for rocky points, shell beds, bluff walls. Structure-specific but not standard condition fields.

suggestions.originalText: FLW pro Casey Martin: Pair stick bait with monofilament leader on C-rig — lets bait float above short grass on ledges and offshore points for appearance of vulnerable baitfish
suggestions.reasoning: 'Ledges and offshore points' implies deeper water. Moderate confidence.

suggestions.originalText: Hawk (pro): Yamamoto Stretch 40 grub works on Carolina rig, split shot, drop shot, weightless, and flipping
suggestions.reasoning: Versatility tip — works on many rigs. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yama Craw on Carolina rig floats entire bait up out of silt and off the bottom, putting it right in face of bass on the prowl
suggestions.reasoning: Buoyancy technique for C-rig. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Schultz: Rig a 4-inch Senko wacky-style on a drop shot to outfish anglers throwing unweighted stickbaits — 'it was just a different look' that caught larger bass in a community hole
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — wacky Senko on drop shot. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Fukae: Fish a drop-shot on baitcast gear — easier to work the bait and cast than spinning; more common technique in Japan
suggestions.reasoning: Gear preference — baitcast for drop shot. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Nose hook or Texas rig a 3-inch stickbait for simple baitfish profile. For power shotting, upsize to 4-5 inch wacky rigged for maximum motion
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging options for different situations. No specific conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Hite: 'The Shad Shape worm is perfect for nose-hooking on a drop-shot. Up north it's a smallmouth catching machine. I use it anytime I'm vertically fishing a drop-shot'
suggestions.reasoning: Bait preference for vertical drop-shot and smallmouth. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Howell (pro): Yamamoto/Daiwa Neko Straight 6.5-inch is excellent as drop-shot worm and for skipping under docks
suggestions.reasoning: Bait recommendation. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Fukae: Insert a 1/16-oz G7 Tungsten Screw Sinker Nail Weight into the Senko/Kut Tail and add a G7 Worm Protect Tube to preserve the plastic; pair with a Shimano Expride 172 ML baitcast rod and 8-12lb fluorocarbon
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging details — nail weight, hook, rod, line. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: Neko rig for faster fall with bottom feeding presentation — optionally add VMC Neko Skirt for pizazz
suggestions.reasoning: General bank fishing tip about neko rig. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Japanese pro Iwahori tip (from Yamamoto ranch): Insert nail weight in head but position hook in-line with bait (shank facing forward) instead of perpendicular — allows fishing through vegetation with far less snagging
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique for fishing through vegetation. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Hudson: 'Dinger durability makes it preferred for Neko rigging since inserting nail weight tears softer baits'
suggestions.reasoning: Bait durability preference. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Howell (pro): Daiwa/Yamamoto Neko Macho and 6.5-inch Neko Straight are made of same Senko material but unique designs give different action and fall rate
suggestions.reasoning: Product comparison. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Howell (pro): 'Neko Macho is very versatile - fish it Texas-rigged on Daiichi Xpoint X-25 4/0 HD hook like a Senko and also wacky rig'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging versatility. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto pro Shryock: 'The 2.5-inch Yamatanuki on a ball head or wobble head falls and spirals like a tube — big deal for smallmouth'
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — Yamatanuki on ball head for smallmouth. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Bank fishing tip: Senko on shaky head is good for probing dock pilings, culvert edges, riprap points and bridge pilings
suggestions.reasoning: Bank fishing tip — Senko on shaky head around structure. No standard conditions.

suggestions.originalText: FLW pro Casey Martin: Rig 3/16-oz shaky head with 5-inch stick bait — stands upright for enticing look. Pitch around docks or fish over deeper structure
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging and presentation technique. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Senko on 3/16oz jighead is great finesse tactic for pressured waters or postspawn. Let it fall next to dock, shake in place, then retrieve
suggestions.reasoning: Explicitly mentions 'postspawn' and 'pressured waters'. Post-spawn is a clear seasonal condition.

suggestions.originalText: For Yamamoto 10-inch grub: use Gamakatsu EWG Superline 5/0 hook with screw-in 1/4oz weight (F rig) and delayed hookset. Straight shank hooks cause missed fish
suggestions.reasoning: Rigging technique — hook and weight selection. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Slow presentation and work the 10-inch grub methodically on deep grass beds for best results
suggestions.reasoning: 'Deep grass beds' implies deeper water, min ~12ft.

suggestions.originalText: Yamamoto Hula Grub and Double Tail Grub are staples for football heads and as jig trailers
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer recommendation — grubs for football heads. No conditions.

suggestions.originalText: Yama Craw buoyancy makes claws float up behind football jig in defensive crawfish posture. Perfect for bottom-hopping presentations
suggestions.reasoning: Trailer buoyancy technique. No conditions.
